quiz_questions.question_text: What is chromatography used for?

options.text: Combining two substances into a single new compound
Separating the components of a mixture from each other
Measuring the mass of chemicals in a laboratory sample
Heating a substance until it changes into a different state

quiz_questions.explanation: Chromatography is a technique used to separate the individual components of a mixture based on how they interact with a stationary phase and a mobile phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: In paper chromatography, what carries the mixture up the paper?

options.text: Gravity pulling the mixture downward through the paper
A solvent that travels up the paper by capillary action
Heat applied from below that pushes molecules upward
Static electricity that attracts molecules toward the top

quiz_questions.explanation: In paper chromatography, a solvent (the mobile phase) moves up the paper through capillary action, carrying the dissolved components of the mixture along with it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do different components of a mixture travel different distances on chromatography paper?

options.text: Heavier molecules are pushed further by the solvent than lighter ones
Each component interacts differently with the paper and the solvent
The temperature at the top of the paper is different from the bottom
All components actually travel the same distance on the paper strip

quiz_questions.explanation: Each component has a different affinity for the stationary phase (paper) and the mobile phase (solvent). Components more attracted to the solvent travel farther; those more attracted to the paper stay behind.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the stationary phase in paper chromatography?

options.text: The solvent that moves up the paper strip
The mixture dot placed on the paper strip
The paper itself that does not move at all
The container holding the solvent below it

quiz_questions.explanation: In paper chromatography, the paper is the stationary phase — it stays in place while the solvent (mobile phase) moves through it, carrying components at different rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the mobile phase in paper chromatography?

options.text: The paper strip used as the medium
The solvent that moves through the paper
The pencil line drawn on the paper strip
The ink dot placed at the starting line

quiz_questions.explanation: The mobile phase is the solvent (often water or alcohol) that moves up through the paper by capillary action, carrying dissolved substances with it at different rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a chromatography experiment, a student finds that black ink separates into blue, red, and yellow bands. What does this tell you about the ink?

options.text: The black ink is a pure substance made of a single dye compound
The black ink is a mixture of at least three different colored dyes
The paper reacted chemically with the ink to create new substances
The solvent changed the color of the ink by dissolving its molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: The separation into multiple colored bands shows that the black ink is a mixture of different dyes. Chromatography physically separated them based on their different properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is the starting line in paper chromatography drawn with pencil instead of pen?

options.text: Pencil graphite is insoluble in the solvent and will not interfere with results
Pencil marks are darker and easier to see on the chromatography paper strip
Pen ink is too thick and would clog the pores of the chromatography paper strip
Pencil marks help the solvent travel up the paper more quickly and efficiently

quiz_questions.explanation: Pencil graphite does not dissolve in the solvent, so it will not move up the paper and interfere with the results. Pen ink would dissolve and travel, contaminating the experiment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens if the solvent level is above the sample spot when starting a chromatography experiment?

options.text: The sample dissolves directly into the solvent and washes off the paper strip
The experiment will work better because more solvent reaches the sample faster
The paper will dissolve in the solvent and the experiment must be redone again
Nothing different happens because the solvent level does not matter at all here

quiz_questions.explanation: If the sample spot is submerged in the solvent, the sample will dissolve directly into the solvent reservoir instead of traveling up the paper. The spot must be above the solvent level.

quiz_questions.question_text: A component that is very attracted to the solvent (mobile phase) will travel __________ on the paper.

options.text: a short distance near the starting line
a long distance up toward the top edge
sideways across the paper to the right
downward below the original starting line

quiz_questions.explanation: Components that are more attracted to the mobile phase (solvent) dissolve more readily in it and travel farther up the paper. Those more attracted to the paper stay closer to the starting line.

quiz_questions.question_text: What value is used to identify substances in chromatography?

options.text: The color of the separated spots on the paper
The Rf (retention factor) value for each spot
The temperature of the solvent during the test
The width of each band that appears on the paper

quiz_questions.explanation: The Rf (retention factor) value is calculated by dividing the distance traveled by a component by the distance traveled by the solvent front. Each substance has a characteristic Rf value.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Rf value in chromatography?

options.text: The ratio of the distance a component travels to the distance the solvent front travels
The total distance traveled by the solvent from the bottom to the top of the paper strip
The total number of different components that are found in a particular mixture sample
The time required for the solvent to reach the top edge of the chromatography paper strip

quiz_questions.explanation: The Rf (retention factor) value equals the distance traveled by a substance divided by the distance traveled by the solvent front. It is always a number between 0 and 1.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'stationary phase'?

options.text: The part of the chromatography system that remains fixed and does not move
The liquid solvent that carries the mixture components through the system
The sample mixture that is placed on the starting line of the paper strip
The container that holds the solvent at the bottom of the chromatography setup

quiz_questions.explanation: The stationary phase is the part of the chromatography system that does not move. In paper chromatography, the paper is the stationary phase.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'mobile phase'?

options.text: The fixed material that the mixture components interact with and stick to
The moving solvent that carries the mixture components through the system
The pencil line drawn at the bottom of the paper as a starting reference
The colored spots that appear on the paper after the experiment is done

quiz_questions.explanation: The mobile phase is the solvent that moves through the stationary phase, carrying dissolved mixture components with it. Different components travel at different rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'capillary action' mean?

options.text: The ability of a liquid to flow upward through narrow spaces against gravity
The process of a gas dissolving into a liquid when pressure is increased
The downward flow of liquid through a filter paper due to gravity alone
The evaporation of a liquid from the surface of a warm container walls

quiz_questions.explanation: Capillary action is the ability of a liquid to flow upward in narrow spaces (like the fibers in paper) against gravity. This is what drives the solvent up the chromatography paper.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'solvent front' mean in chromatography?

options.text: The furthest point that the solvent has traveled up the paper at any given time
The starting line where the sample mixture was originally placed on the paper
The bottom of the paper strip where it contacts the solvent in the container
The side of the paper that faces the front of the chromatography setup container

quiz_questions.explanation: The solvent front is the leading edge of the solvent as it moves up the paper. It marks the maximum distance the solvent has traveled and is used to calculate Rf values.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'affinity' mean in the context of chromatography?

options.text: The attraction or tendency of a substance to interact with a particular phase
The speed at which a solvent travels up the chromatography paper strip now
The total number of colored spots visible on the paper after the experiment
The temperature needed to dissolve a substance in the mobile phase solvent

quiz_questions.explanation: Affinity refers to how strongly a substance is attracted to or interacts with a particular phase. A substance with high affinity for the mobile phase travels far; high affinity for the stationary phase means it stays near the start.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'chromatogram'?

options.text: The resulting pattern of separated components on the paper after the experiment
The equipment setup used to perform the chromatography experiment in a lab
The mathematical formula used to calculate the speed of the solvent movement
The type of solvent selected for use in a particular chromatography experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: A chromatogram is the visual result of a chromatography experiment — the paper (or other medium) showing the pattern of separated spots or bands from the original mixture.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'pigment' mean?

options.text: A substance that gives color to a material by absorbing specific wavelengths of light
A chemical compound that is always colorless and invisible to the unaided human eye
The type of paper specifically designed for use in chromatography experiments only
A liquid that is used as the mobile phase in a paper chromatography separation test

quiz_questions.explanation: A pigment is a substance that absorbs certain wavelengths of light and reflects others, giving materials their color. Chromatography can separate mixtures of different pigments.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'soluble' mean?

options.text: Able to be seen with the naked eye without any magnification aid
Able to dissolve in a particular solvent to form a clear solution
Able to be heated until it changes from a solid into a gas state
Able to be separated by using a magnet to attract its particles

quiz_questions.explanation: Soluble means that a substance can dissolve in a particular solvent. In chromatography, the components must be at least partially soluble in the mobile phase to be carried along.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'separation technique' mean?

options.text: A method used to combine two or more substances into a single new compound
A method used to isolate individual components from a mixture of substances
A method used to determine the temperature of a mixture in a science lab
A method used to change the state of matter of a substance through heating

quiz_questions.explanation: A separation technique is any method used to divide a mixture into its individual components. Chromatography, filtration, distillation, and evaporation are all examples.

quiz_questions.question_text: A police forensic lab receives two ransom notes written with black markers. They want to determine if both notes were written with the same marker. How can chromatography help?

options.text: By testing if the ink from each note produces the same chromatogram pattern
By heating the ink on each note to see which one evaporates first in the lab
By dissolving both notes in acid to determine their total chemical composition
By weighing the ink on each note to see if the mass of the ink is the same

quiz_questions.explanation: Chromatography can separate the dyes in each ink sample. If both produce identical chromatogram patterns (same number of bands, same colors, same Rf values), the inks are likely the same.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student performs chromatography on three different green food colorings and gets different patterns. What can they conclude?

options.text: All three are the same substance because they are all the same green color
The three colorings contain different combinations of dyes mixed together
The experiment failed because green food coloring cannot be separated at all
The paper reacted differently with each coloring causing false results here

quiz_questions.explanation: Different chromatogram patterns mean the green colorings contain different combinations of dyes. Even though they look the same color, they are made from different mixtures of individual dyes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a spot of ink at the bottom of chromatography paper and dips the paper into water. After 15 minutes, one dye has traveled 6 cm and the solvent front traveled 10 cm. What is the Rf value of that dye?
quiz_questions.explanation: Rf = distance traveled by component / distance traveled by solvent front = 6 cm / 10 cm = 0.60. This Rf value can help identify the dye by comparison with known values.

quiz_questions.question_text: A leaf is crushed and its pigments are separated by chromatography. Multiple colored bands appear: green, yellow, and orange. What does this tell you about leaves?

options.text: Leaves only contain chlorophyll, and the other colors are created by the solvent
Leaves contain multiple pigments, but green chlorophyll is usually most visible
The chromatography experiment caused chemical reactions that created new pigments
Only old or dying leaves contain multiple pigments while healthy ones have just green

quiz_questions.explanation: Leaves contain multiple pigments including chlorophyll (green), carotenoids (orange), and xanthophylls (yellow). Chromatography reveals these hidden pigments that are normally masked by the dominant green.

quiz_questions.question_text: A candy company claims its brown candy coating uses only one dye. How could a student test this claim?

options.text: Dissolve the coating and use chromatography to see how many dyes separate out
Heat the candy until it melts and see if the color changes to a different shade now
Weigh the candy before and after eating it to determine the mass of dye it contains
Look at the candy under a microscope to count the number of dye particles visible

quiz_questions.explanation: By dissolving the brown coating and running chromatography, the student can see if one or multiple dyes separate. If multiple bands appear, the brown is a mixture of dyes, not one dye.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses water as the solvent for chromatography but gets poor separation. The teacher suggests using rubbing alcohol instead. Why might a different solvent give better results?

options.text: Different solvents interact differently with the dyes, improving the separation between them
Rubbing alcohol travels faster up the paper, so the experiment completes more quickly overall
Alcohol chemically changes the dyes into different substances that are easier to see on paper
Water is too heavy to move up the paper properly, but alcohol is light enough to travel up

quiz_questions.explanation: Different solvents have different abilities to dissolve and carry the components. Some dyes dissolve better in alcohol than water, allowing better separation of the mixture components.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two students perform chromatography on the same ink but get slightly different Rf values. What could explain this difference?

options.text: The ink changed its chemical composition between the two separate experiments
Minor differences in temperature, paper type, or technique could affect the results
Rf values are always random and never reproduce the same results twice in a row
One student used the wrong formula to calculate the Rf value for the experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: Small variations in experimental conditions — like temperature, paper brand, amount of sample, or solvent composition — can cause slight differences in Rf values between trials.

quiz_questions.question_text: A food scientist needs to check if an imported spice contains illegal artificial dyes. Which analytical technique would be most useful?

options.text: Filtration to separate the solid spice particles from any dye present
Evaporation to see if any colored residue remains after all water is gone
Chromatography to separate and identify each dye component in the spice
Magnetism to pull out any metallic dye compounds found within the spice

quiz_questions.explanation: Chromatography can separate the dye components in the spice extract and compare their Rf values to known artificial dyes. This reveals whether illegal additives are present.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wonders why some colored spots on their chromatogram are larger and more spread out than others. What might cause this?

options.text: Those dyes are present in larger amounts in the original ink mixture sample
Those dyes are chemically reacting with the paper and expanding on contact
The solvent pushed those particular dyes sideways rather than straight upward
Those spots are actually errors and should be ignored when analyzing results

quiz_questions.explanation: Larger, more spread-out spots typically indicate that the dye is present in a greater amount in the original mixture. The spot size can give qualitative information about the concentration of each component.

quiz_questions.question_text: A biologist uses chromatography to study amino acids in a protein sample, but the amino acids are colorless. How can they see the separated spots?

options.text: They cannot see them; chromatography only works with colored substances in all cases
They can spray the paper with a staining chemical that reacts with amino acids to show color
They heat the paper until the invisible spots start to glow from the absorbed thermal energy
They weigh different sections of the paper to find where the heavier amino acids gathered

quiz_questions.explanation: Colorless substances can be made visible by spraying the chromatogram with a developing agent (like ninhydrin for amino acids) that reacts with the components to produce colored spots.
